confidence.disclosure: Rumi's life is well documented by his son Sultan Walad, by his student and scribe Husam al-Din Chalabi, and by the early hagiographer Aflaki. The two pivotal dates — the meeting with Shams of Tabriz in Konya on 15 November 1244 and Rumi's own death on 17 December 1273 — are reported to the day. The intermediate stops on the family's long migration west from Balkh (Samarkand, Nishapur, Baghdad, Mecca, Damascus, Erzincan, Larende, Konya) are unanimous in the sources but the years of each stop are sometimes given in ranges of 1216–1228 because the family was on the road for over a decade. The Attar story at Nishapur is a tradition rather than verified history but is reported in every early account.

sources: Sultan Walad, Ibtida-nama (the son's verse memoir)
Shams al-Din Aflaki, Manaqib al-Arifin (early 14th c. hagiography)
Annemarie Schimmel, The Triumphal Sun: A Study of Rumi's Works (1978)
Franklin D. Lewis, Rumi: Past and Present, East and West (2000)

journey.event: Born on 30 September 1207 in Balkh (or possibly Wakhsh in modern Tajikistan, culturally part of greater Balkh) to Baha al-Din Walad, a noted theologian, jurist and Sufi preacher.
The family moves from Balkh to Samarkand, possibly due to a dispute between Baha al-Din and the local ruler. Young Jalal al-Din spends some childhood years here on the eve of the Mongol storm.
With the Mongol armies of Genghis Khan advancing across Central Asia, Baha al-Din decides to leave Khurasan for good. The family sets out on what will become a fifteen-year migration to the west.

The family passes through Nishapur. According to a beloved tradition, the elderly mystical poet Farid al-Din Attar meets the young Rumi, recognises his future greatness, and presents him with a copy of his Ilahi-nama, saying to Baha al-Din: 'This boy will set fire to the world.'
The family continues west through Baghdad on its way to the holy cities, joining the great pilgrim route.
Performs the pilgrimage with his father.

Spends time in Damascus on the family's slow westward journey. Damascus at this moment is the home of Ibn Arabi, who according to one tradition saw the boy walking behind his father and remarked: 'Glory be to God — an ocean is following a lake.'
The family reaches Anatolia — the land called Rum, the old Eastern Roman territory now ruled by the Seljuk Sultanate. They settle for a time in Erzincan in eastern Anatolia.
Living in Larende (modern Karaman) under Seljuk protection. Rumi marries Gowhar Khatun here. His mother dies in Larende, and his first son, Sultan Walad, is born.
The Seljuk sultan Ala al-Din Kayqubad invites Baha al-Din to the capital, Konya. The family settles there permanently. Baha al-Din teaches at one of the city's many madrasas.
His father dies. Rumi inherits his teaching position and becomes head of the madrasa, taking up the family vocation as scholar, jurist and preacher in the Hanafi school.

Travels to Aleppo and then to Damascus to deepen his studies in jurisprudence and theology, possibly spending several years in Syria. Continues his Sufi training under his father's former student Burhan al-Din Muhaqqiq Tirmidhi.

Burhan al-Din dies. Rumi's nine-year period of formal Sufi training under him is complete. He is now a fully-formed scholar and jurist, issuing fatwas and giving sermons in the great mosques of Konya.

On 15 November 1244, in the bazaar of Konya, Rumi meets the wandering dervish Shams al-Din of Tabriz. The encounter — over a question Shams asks him about the difference between Bayazid's intoxicated love and the Prophet's sober knowledge — shatters his world. The accomplished jurist becomes an ecstatic. The two retreat into months of unbroken spiritual conversation.
Rumi's family and disciples, jealous of Shams's hold over him, force Shams to leave Konya in February 1246. Rumi is heartbroken and refuses to teach.

Rumi sends his son Sultan Walad to find Shams in Damascus and bring him back. The two are reunited in Konya later that year and fall at each other's feet, so that 'no one knew who was the lover and who the beloved'.

On the night of 5 December 1248, while Rumi and Shams are talking, Shams is called to the back door of the house. He goes out and is never seen again. Rumi refuses for forty days to believe the rumours of Shams's death; modern scholarship considers it likely that Shams was murdered, possibly with the knowledge of one of Rumi's own sons.

The years of pouring out poetry. The Divan-e Shams — over 30,000 verses of ghazals and quatrains addressed to and signed in the name of his lost beloved — is composed in this period. Rumi finds new mirrors for the friendship in the goldsmith Salah al-Din Zarkub and later in his scribe Husam al-Din Chalabi.

At Husam al-Din's suggestion, Rumi begins dictating the Masnavi-yi Ma'navi, the six-volume mystical epic later called 'the Qur'an in Persian'. He continues to dictate it until shortly before his death.

Dies in Konya on 17 December 1273 — a date his followers still call Sheb-i Arus, the Wedding Night, the night of his union with the Beloved. His funeral is attended by Muslims, Christians and Jews of the city alike. His tomb at Konya remains one of the most visited shrines in the Islamic world.
